title: The Succession Challenge for China's Family Businesses
content:
Family businesses are the backbone of China's economy, accounting for over 80 percent of private firms since the late 1970s. The private sector is vital to the nation, generating over half of all tax revenue, two-thirds of the GDP, and 80 percent of urban employment. As a result, the succession of these companies from founders to their children is not merely a business matter, but a profound social issue. Many of these heirs, known as 'changerdai,' are only children who were educated overseas and exposed to very different cultures. Returning to run family factories in remote towns often brings a jarring reverse culture shock.

For a generation shaped by a stronger sense of individual identity, taking over the family firm is viewed more as a process of self-discovery than an unquestioned obligation. This fresh perspective helps explain their strong appetite for modernizing and innovating their inherited businesses. Many younger leaders have turned to livestreaming, aggressive overseas expansion, and cultivating personal brands on social media to drive growth. These varied experiments in succession illustrate how individual firms are adapting to a rapidly changing business environment. Ultimately, they also reflect broader, systemic shifts across China’s evolving industries and consumer markets.
